summary: Tanzih is the theological affirmation of God's transcendence and incomparability, declaring God free of the deficiencies, limitations, and likeness that belong to created things. It is standardly paired with, and contrasted against, tashbih, the likening of God to creatures.
description:
Tanzih derives from a root meaning to declare something pure or free of defect, and in theology denotes purifying the conception of God from any resemblance to creation and from attributes proper to creatures such as body, place, or change. It draws on Qur'anic language of God's uniqueness, especially the statement that "there is nothing like unto Him" (Q 42:11), and expresses the negative or apophatic side of the doctrine of divine oneness.

Tanzih is generally treated as the counterpart of tashbih (assimilation or anthropomorphism), which carried a pejorative sense as describing God in human terms. The tension between the two was among the formative issues of early and classical kalam. The Mu'tazila are associated with a strong emphasis on transcendence, extending tanzih to a figurative reading of the revealed "ambiguous" attributes so as to remove any anthropomorphic implication. Traditionalist and Hanbali currents worried that an unchecked tanzih could empty the scriptural descriptions of God of meaning (a charge they termed taʿṭīl, "divesting"), and preferred to affirm the attributes as revealed while denying likeness. The Ash'ari and Maturidi schools sought intermediate formulations that affirmed real attributes while maintaining that God does not resemble His creation.

The balance between asserting transcendence and affirming the descriptions God gives of Himself remained a persistent axis of theological debate; this entry sets out the positions without endorsing any.
editorial_notes: Scope is the classical kalam framing of tanzih as the pair-opposite of tashbih and as the apophatic pole against the danger of taʿṭīl. The association of particular tendencies with particular schools (Mu'tazila with strong tanzih and taʾwīl, Hanbali/Athari with affirmation-without-likeness) is a standard summary that compresses internal variation and should not be read as uniform school doctrine. The Sufi elaboration of tanzih and tashbih as complementary rather than opposed (notably in the Akbari/Ibn ʿArabi tradition) is deliberately out of scope here and is a candidate for a separate entry. Q 42:11 is cited via secondary summaries and should be checked against the Qur'an directly. A Muslim/Islamic scholarly primary text is retained for triangulation and balanced coverage (neutrality principles #7 and #8): Ibn Taymiyya's al-ʿAqīda al-Wāsiṭiyya, which states the Athari understanding of tanzīh (affirmation-without-likening, guarding equally against tashbīh and taʿṭīl), cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Per neutrality principle #9 the reference-work and tertiary citations were removed (Encyclopaedia of Islam van Ess, Oxford Bibliographies Shah, Wikipedia); to keep the entry from resting on a single school's primary text, Livnat Holtzman's academic monograph Anthropomorphism in Islam (Edinburgh University Press, 2018) was added, a scholarly treatment covering both the transcendentalist and traditionalist sides of the tanzīh/tashbīh debate. Third-lens update (2026-07-18): a modern Muslim scholarly source was appended, Ramon Harvey's Transcendent God, Rational World: A Māturīdī Theology (Edinburgh University Press, 2021), whose chapter on the Divine Nature reconstructs the Māturīdī account of God's transcendence and the affirmation of the attributes without likening, exactly the tanzīh terrain; verified via the publisher listing, Edinburgh Scholarship Online table of contents, and academic reviews. The entry now triangulates three independent lenses that converge on the same doctrinal picture: Western academic Islamic studies (Holtzman), a classical Muslim primary text (Ibn Taymiyya), and a contemporary Muslim scholar (Harvey); cited at medium confidence as the book was not read line-by-line.

relations.note: Tanzih (incomparability) is standardly opposed to tashbih (likening God to creatures).
Tanzih expresses the negative/apophatic aspect of divine oneness.
How the divine attributes are affirmed depends on preserving tanzih.
Denying God any peer or likeness underlies the rejection of shirk.

historical_period.note: A formative issue of early and classical theology; the transcendentalist position competed for acceptance into roughly the 10th century CE.

sources.citation: Harvey, Ramon. Transcendent God, Rational World: A Māturīdī Theology. Edinburgh Studies in Islamic Scripture and Theology. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press, 2021. Academic monograph by a contemporary Muslim scholar reconstructing Māturīdī theology in dialogue with modern philosophy; its chapter on the Divine Nature treats divine transcendence and the affirmation of the attributes without likening (tanzīh).
sources.license: Open access (OAPEN); also proprietary print. Cited for reference, consulted via publisher listing, table of contents, and reviews, not read in full.

sources.citation: Holtzman, Livnat. Anthropomorphism in Islam: The Challenge of Traditionalism (700-1350). Edinburgh Studies in Classical Islamic History and Culture. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press, 2018. Academic monograph analyzing the traditionalist and rationalist handling of the divine attributes, including the tanzīh/tashbīh tension and the aḥādīth al-ṣifāt.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, consulted via publisher description and reviews, not read in full.

sources.citation: Ibn Taymiyya, Taqī al-Dīn Aḥmad. al-ʿAqīda al-Wāsiṭiyya. Classical Athari/traditionist creed holding that God's revealed names and attributes are incomparable (tanzīh) and must be affirmed without likening (tashbīh/tamthīl) or divesting them of meaning (taʿṭīl).
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
